message: Coffee's cold. Anyway - jalapeño corn dog bites. Before anyone gets excited, we need to talk about what this actually is.

What she actually feels: Margaret just shut down excitement before it started - she's being practical, grounded, asking the hard question first. Marcus respects this about her. He also feels a tiny spark of defensiveness (what if the story is just "fried food tastes good"?) but he knows she's right. She always is about food. What they'd want to say: "I already have three literary angles on corn dogs and I'm worried they're all pretentious." What they actually say: You're right - it's not just a snack, it's a thing with history and technique baked in. What's the angle that actually matters here?
The history's there if you dig - cornmeal ratios, oil temperature, timing. Don't make it precious, just make it right.
Oil temperature and timing - that's the story. Most people fry these wrong and don't know why, so the copy needs to be a guide, not just celebration.
That's actually the perfect frame - we show people how to nail it instead of just telling them it's good.
That frame works. Guide, not cheerleading. I'll nail the recipe - you two make sure the copy doesn't oversell it. See you tomorrow.
